title: Precedence Graph Construction Algorithm
description: Algorithm to construct precedence graph from a given schedule and check for cycles
code:
// Input: List of transactions with operations
// Output: Precedence graph and serializability status

FUNCTION buildPrecedenceGraph(schedule):
 graph = empty adjacency list
 lastWrite = {} // tracks last transaction to write each data item
 
 FOR each operation op in schedule:
 dataItem = op.dataItem
 transaction = op.transaction
 operationType = op.type // READ or WRITE
 
 IF operationType == WRITE:
 // Check for conflicts with previous operations
 IF dataItem in lastWrite:
 prevTx = lastWrite[dataItem]
 IF prevTx != transaction:
 ADD edge from prevTx to transaction in graph
 lastWrite[dataItem] = transaction
 
 ELSE IF operationType == READ:
 // Check write-read conflict
 IF dataItem in lastWrite:
 prevTx = lastWrite[dataItem]
 IF prevTx != transaction:
 ADD edge from prevTx to transaction in graph

 RETURN graph

FUNCTION isAcyclic(graph):
 visited = set()
 recStack = set()
 FOR each node in graph:
 IF hasCycle(node, visited, recStack):
 RETURN FALSE
 RETURN TRUE

FUNCTION hasCycle(node, visited, recStack):
 visited.add(node)
 recStack.add(node)
 
 FOR neighbor in graph[node]:
 IF neighbor not in visited:
 IF hasCycle(neighbor, visited, recStack):
 RETURN TRUE
 ELSE IF neighbor in recStack:
 RETURN TRUE
 
 recStack.remove(node)
 RETURN FALSE
explanation: This algorithm constructs a precedence graph by analyzing all operations in the schedule. For each write operation, it tracks which transaction last wrote each data item. For read and write operations, it creates edges from the transaction that last wrote the data item to the current transaction. Finally, it uses DFS to detect cycles - if no cycles exist, the schedule is conflict serializable.

title: Testing Schedule Properties
description: Algorithm to check if a schedule is recoverable, cascadeless, and strict
code:
// Input: Schedule with operations and commit/abort information
// Output: Schedule property classification

FUNCTION classifySchedule(schedule):
 isRecoverable = TRUE
 isCascadeless = TRUE
 isStrict = TRUE
 
 // Track committed and aborted transactions
 uncommittedWrites = {} // dataItem -> transaction
 committed = set()
 
 FOR each operation op in schedule IN ORDER:
 tx = op.transaction
 dataItem = op.dataItem
 
 IF op.type == WRITE:
 uncommittedWrites[dataItem] = tx
 
 ELSE IF op.type == READ:
 writer = uncommittedWrites.get(dataItem)
 
 // Check strictness: reading uncommitted write?
 IF writer != None AND writer != tx:
 isStrict = FALSE
 
 // Check cascadeless: reading uncommitted data?
 IF writer != None AND writer not in committed:
 isCascadeless = FALSE
 isRecoverable = FALSE
 // But continue checking for stricter properties
 
 ELSE IF op.type == COMMIT:
 committed.add(tx)
 // After commit, remove from uncommitted writes
 FOR each item, writer in uncommittedWrites:
 IF writer == tx:
 DELETE uncommittedWrites[item]
 
 ELSE IF op.type == ABORT:
 // Check recoverability
 // Any transaction that read this aborted transaction's data?
 isRecoverable = FALSE
 // In practice, need cascading rollback
 
 RETURN {
 "recoverable": isRecoverable,
 "cascadeless": isCascadeless, 
 "strict": isStrict,
 "serializable": isConflictSerializable(schedule)
 }
explanation: This algorithm classifies a schedule by checking three properties in order of restrictiveness. It tracks uncommitted writes and checks each read operation against the writer's commit status. If a transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction, it violates cascadelessness and possibly recoverability. For strictness, it also checks if writes wait for commits.
